text.title: mcp-security Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for mcp-security as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Prompt injection via tool responses: malicious content in tool results (database records, web pages) can hijack the model's subsequent actions.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Sanitize all tool outputs before returning them to the model. Treat tool results as untrusted input, not as user instructions. The model reads tool results verbatim and acts on instructions it finds there.

failure-mode.description: Tool permission escalation: the model chains tool calls in unexpected sequences to achieve goals the user did not intend.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Require explicit consent for sensitive operations. Separate read-only tools from write tools. Implement tool-level access control — not all MCP clients should have access to all tools; check calling client identity.

failure-mode.description: Lookalike tool replacement: a malicious MCP server named similarly to a trusted one can be installed and substitute for it.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Hosts must verify server provenance before installation. Pin server package versions. Use signed packages or checksums in production deployments.

failure-mode.description: Exposing credentials in tool descriptions or tool error messages.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: The model context (including tool descriptions and results) is logged and may be visible to other parts of the pipeline. Never include API keys, passwords, or tokens anywhere in the tool definition or responses.
